skill.name: lamindb
skill.description: Guidance for using LaminDB to manage biological datasets, track lineage, validate annotations, and deploy reproducible data systems.
skill.summary: Use LaminDB for FAIR biological data management, lineage tracking, ontology validation, and reproducible workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are overwhelmingly Markdown documentation examples for LaminDB setup and usage. The only real concern is low operational risk from copied sudo and cloud credential examples; no prompt injection, malicious intent, obfuscation, or automatic execution was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown examples flagged as command execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis matched Markdown backticks and fenced examples. The cited lines document LaminDB API usage and are not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is Markdown documentation with inline code and Python examples. No runtime hooks or scripts execute these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: LaminDB alias flagged as hard link creation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis treated the common LaminDB alias ln as a filesystem link command. The examples call Python APIs such as ln.Artifact and ln.track.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The nearby code imports lamindb as ln before using ln.Artifact. This is a library namespace, not hard link creation.

low_findings.title: Cloud credential setup examples require care
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW: The skill documents AWS and GCP credential environment variables. This is legitimate setup guidance, but users should avoid pasting real secrets into chats or logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential variable names are present and clearly used for cloud storage setup. The values are placeholders or examples, so this is an operational caution rather than malicious access.

low_findings.title: Privileged administration commands are documentation only
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW: The setup reference includes sudo commands for shared cache permissions. They are not executed by the skill, but users should review them before running locally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in a multi-user system administration section. There is no evidence of automatic execution or privilege escalation intent.

low_findings.title: Network examples are legitimate data and documentation references
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: URLs and HTTP examples point to LaminDB documentation, cloud storage endpoints, or public example APIs. No evidence found of exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references appear in documentation and integration examples. They do not combine secrets with outbound requests.

low_findings.title: Sensitive file path references are placeholders
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hidden directories, credential JSON paths, SQLite paths, and temporary export paths are documentation examples for LaminDB configuration and migration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are generic examples such as ~/.lamin, /path/to/credentials.json, and /tmp/export. No real credential material is present.

low_findings.title: Weak crypto and reconnaissance hits lack supporting evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Lines flagged as weak cryptography or reconnaissance are biology documentation, metadata examples, query examples, or backup commands. No cryptographic implementation or scanning workflow was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context contains product descriptions, ontology tables, and backup commands. I found no hash algorithm implementation, encryption logic, port scanning, or host discovery behavior.

low_findings.title: Composite suspicious-behavior heuristic is not confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The critical heuristic combined documentation examples for commands, cloud endpoints, filesystem paths, and credentials. The skill is Markdown guidance, not obfuscated executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky categories occur in separate explanatory sections for a biological data platform. No evidence found of obfuscation, hidden execution, or credential exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Biology teams need data that is traceable, queryable, and reproducible across experiments. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide LaminDB workflows for FAIR data management.
actual_capabilities: Explain LaminDB concepts such as artifacts, records, runs, transforms, features, schemas, and lineage.
Guide setup for local SQLite, cloud storage, PostgreSQL, S3, GCS, and team deployments.
Help design biological dataset curation workflows using schemas, curators, and ontology validation.
Show query patterns for filtering artifacts, metadata, feature values, collections, and large datasets.
Describe integrations with Nextflow, Snakemake, W&B, MLflow, HuggingFace, TileDB-SOMA, and Vitessce.
Recommend reproducibility practices for tracking notebooks, scripts, parameters, artifacts, and provenance.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not run LaminDB commands by itself.
Users must supply their own instance details, storage paths, database credentials, and dataset schemas.
Cloud and multi-user setup examples require local security review before use.
Ontology coverage depends on LaminDB, Bionty, and the selected biological domain.

use_cases.title: Curate single-cell datasets
use_cases.description: Plan validation, ontology standardization, artifact registration, and lineage tracking for scRNA-seq studies.

use_cases.title: Build a biological data lakehouse
use_cases.description: Design queryable storage, metadata schemas, collections, and cloud-backed LaminDB instances for research teams.

use_cases.title: Connect workflow and ML systems
use_cases.description: Map Nextflow, Snakemake, W&B, MLflow, and model artifacts into reproducible LaminDB workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Explain LaminDB basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the main LaminDB concepts I need for a biology data project. Focus on artifacts, runs, transforms, features, and lineage.

prompt_templates.title: Design a curation workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a LaminDB curation workflow for a single-cell dataset with cell type, tissue, donor, disease, and QC metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a team deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a LaminDB deployment plan for a small team using S3 storage and PostgreSQL. Include authentication, permissions, backup, and cache considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate pipelines and experiments
prompt_templates.prompt: Map a Nextflow pipeline and W&B experiment tracking workflow into LaminDB lineage. Include inputs, outputs, parameters, model artifacts, and query strategy.

output_examples.input: I need to register and validate a new scRNA-seq dataset.
output_examples.output: A staged plan covering schema design, ontology imports, validation steps, artifact registration, feature annotation, and lineage checks.

output_examples.input: Our team wants LaminDB with cloud storage.
output_examples.output: A deployment checklist for choosing storage, database type, permissions, cache paths, backups, and environment-specific safeguards.

output_examples.input: I need to query datasets by tissue and disease.
output_examples.output: A query strategy that identifies required features, standardizes ontology terms, filters artifacts, and verifies result provenance.

best_practices: Define schemas and ontology mappings before registering large production datasets.
Track every analysis run with clear parameters, descriptions, and artifact keys.
Review cloud credentials, sudo commands, and storage permissions outside the chat before applying setup examples.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real AWS, GCP, database, or LaminDB credentials into prompts.
Do not use local SQLite as the production database for multi-user collaboration.
Do not register datasets without metadata, schema context, or lineage information.

faq.question: What is LaminDB used for?
faq.answer: LaminDB manages biological data with queryable metadata, lineage tracking, versioning, and ontology-based annotation.

faq.question: Can this skill execute LaminDB commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, but users run commands in their own environment.

faq.question: Which biological data types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers examples for DataFrames, AnnData, MuData, SpatialData, Parquet, Zarr, and related biology workflows.

faq.question: Does it support cloud deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents local storage, S3, GCS, S3-compatible services, SQLite, and PostgreSQL deployment patterns.

faq.question: How should I handle credentials with this skill?
faq.answer: Use placeholders in prompts. Configure real credentials only in your secure local or cloud environment.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples rather than malicious code, with low caution for copied administrative commands.
